title: Understanding the Pythagorean Theorem
text: The Pythagorean Theorem is a fundamental principle in geometry that relates the lengths of the sides of a right triangle. It states that in a right triangle, the square of the length of the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle) is equal to the sum of the squares of the lengths of the other two sides.

title: Identifying the Right Triangle
text: To use the Pythagorean Theorem, you need to identify the right triangle in the problem. Look for a triangle with a 90-degree angle. The side opposite this angle is the hypotenuse.

title: Setting Up the Equation
text: If you have a right triangle with sides a, $$b$$, and hypotenuse c, the Pythagorean Theorem can be written as $$a^2+b^2=c^2$$. Substitute the known values into this equation.

title: Solving for the Unknown Side
text: Once you have set up the equation, solve for the unknown side by isolating it on one side of the equation. You may need to take the square root to find the length of the side.

title: Example Calculation
text: Suppose you have a right triangle with sides $$3$$ and $$4$$, and you need to find the hypotenuse. Set up the equation as $$3^2+4^2=c^2$$. Calculate the values to find c.
